data.title: These 3 Dividend Stocks Could Double - And Pay You Every Step Of The Way
data.description:
Introduction

Investing in stocks is a good idea.

I believe most of my readers who have been in the market for a long time have found this out by simply monitoring the returns in their portfolios.

However, there's a lot of data that supports this.

Using data since 1872, the American stock market (the S&P 500 was launched in the 1950s) has returned 7.1% per year using the geometric average. The arithmetic average was 8.6%.

The geometric average is the average we usually use, as it simply adds all values and divides them by the total sample size.

The arithmetic average accounts for compounding over time, which makes it great for a massive stock market sample size. Instead of treating each year's return separately, it considers how returns build on each other.

While 7.1% may seem like a mildly disappointing number, as everyone is always talking about generating 10% annual returns by investing in stocks, it is important that this number is adjusted for inflation.

Moreover, while we are currently in a very volatile period, long-term investing is more often fun than annoying, as the market has grown in 69% of all years since 1872. Slightly less than a third of all years saw negative returns.

But wait, there's more!

Since 1872, nobody holding stocks for at least 20 years has ever lost money, as the minimum annualized return for anyone holding during any random 20-year trailing period is 0.5%. Only on a short-term basis, it's fair to say the stock market is like a casino, except that we don't immediately lose our entire investment if we pick the wrong stock.

The same cannot be said about the blackjack tables in a casino.

So, for what it's worth, even on a short-term basis, we have an edge over a casino.

Having said all of this, it takes roughly 7.2 years for our capital to double. This is entirely theoretical and based on pre-inflation numbers.

It's the simple calculation of $100 *1.1^7.2, which is $200.

Adjusted for inflation (the 7% annual return number), that number rises to 10.3 years, which means once every decade, the real purchasing power of our investments doubles if we hold the S&P 500.

If you have more than $500,000 now, you'll be a millionaire in 2035. Again, technically speaking.

That's pretty good, isn't it?

Personally, however, I want more, which is why I usually take bigger risks than the 'average' dividend growth investor.

  • I do not invest in ETFs.
  • I do not care much for sector diversification, as 45% of my portfolio consists of industrials, followed by energy with a 37% weighting.
  • Close to a third of my entire net worth is invested in two landowners, which are Texas Pacific Land (TPL) and LandBridge (LB).

I usually aim for an annual return potential of at least 15% when I invest in companies. To reduce my risks, I only buy my highest-conviction picks at valuations that I consider to be perfect, which is why some of my stocks spend years on my watchlist before I pull the trigger.

Having spent the past few days on secure income stocks, I'm pivoting today to discuss three dividend stocks with the *potential* for much faster growth, doubling well ahead of the average ten years.

These aren't your typical 'safe bets'. Instead, they're higher-volatility 'wild cards' that, I believe, have a very realistic chance at doubling in the years ahead.

Also, even if I'm wrong, I believe these stocks all make for great long-term investments, which makes all of them investments with a great risk/reward.

So, let's start with the one that has grabbed the most headlines.

UnitedHealth (UNH) - The Mega-Cap Dumpsterfire

The implosion of UnitedHealth's stock price is one of the most bizarre things I have ever witnessed, as the company, which still has a market cap of roughly $290 billion, has seen its stock price drop by more than 50% before the latest rebound. This made it the third-worst sell-off in its history.

My most recent article on this company was written on April 17, when I went with the title "UNHappy Ending: How Big Challenges Nuked My UnitedHealth Thesis."

The company, which helped millions of investors reach their financial goals between the Great Financial Crisis and the current sell-off, has been suffering from a cost explosion, which caused investors to fear for the durability of its business model.

The company said it was blindsided by rising medical costs as the first quarter closed, upending a forecast it had affirmed just three months ago.

UnitedHealth fell as much as 20% when US markets opened, the most since Sept. 1999. The guidance cut is rare for UnitedHealth, which typically forecasts conservatively and often raises its outlook as the year progresses. - Bloomberg (emphasis added)

Surging medical costs and sticky inflation have caused the company to miss its earnings expectations for the first time in a decade.

To make matters worse, since then, its CEO has left, and the company is under investigation for potential medical fraud.

As a result, investors aren't sure what to make of the giant, as some want the company to get rid of Optum to reduce its exposure to medical cost inflation.

So should the empire be broken up? Investors aren’t there just yet. Selling off pieces of the juggernaut would be too complicated given how tightly integrated the health services business—known as Optum—is to the insurance side, says Jeff Jonas, a portfolio manager at Gabelli Funds, which owns UnitedHealth shares. Many investors still want to give CEO Stephen Hemsley, the returning leader who built up the company, a chance to fix it. But the blind trust once put on UnitedHealth’s ability to consistently beat earnings estimates is gone—and so is the valuation premium that set it apart. - WSJ (emphasis added)

The problem is that in addition to insuring clients, the company also treats them through Optum. Now, cost inflation is a headwind for both the insurance and healthcare parts of the business.

Also, by being unable to code patients for as many conditions as its doctors see fit, it loses another lever to protect margins, according to The Wall Street Journal.

All of this is bad news. It also explains why I prefer other companies with more pricing power in healthcare, including providers of research equipment like Thermo Fisher Scientific (TMO) and Danaher (DHR).

Nonetheless, I like the value that UNH shares present at current levels a lot.

Currently, the company yields 2.7%. This dividend has a payout ratio of 30% and a five-year CAGR of 14.2%. While dividend growth is likely to come down during these times of turmoil, I have little doubt that it will pick up eventually.

I believe analysts have the same opinion, as FactSet data shows an expected EPS growth recovery to 15% and 14% in 2026 and 2027, respectively. This year is expected to be an outlier with 18% EPS contraction.

As a result, UNH shares trade at less than 13x earnings, a number that's low even if it fails to meet analyst targets in the years ahead.

If we get a return to a 17.7x multiple (its two-decade average) over the next 5-7 years by inclusion of annual EPS growth in the low-to-mid teens, I expect UNH to generate $40 in EPS by 2030. A 17x multiple implies a stock price of $680, more than 210% above the current price.

Aris Water Solutions (ARIS) - So Good, I'm Bringing It Up Again

This feels a little bit like cheating, as I brought up Aris on May 17 as well.

However, after covering the stock back then, I have researched the company daily, as I like it even more than I did back then.

In fact, I like it so much that I am considering buying it despite my already massive investments in Permian Basin landowners.

Personally, I have invested in Texas Pacific Land (TPL) and LandBridge (LB) to gain access to the many opportunities that go beyond oil and gas production in the Permian Basin, the largest oil basin of the United States.

Both companies benefit from rapidly rising water demand for two reasons, which I also brought up in the May 17 article:

  • Unconventional oil and gas production requires water. The average fracking job uses roughly 4 million gallons of water per well. On top of rising oil production, the declining quality of existing reserves requires deeper wells and more complicated structures, which pushes up water demand even if the total rig count remains unchanged.
  • The fracking process also results in the production of brackish/produced water. This water is toxic and needs to be taken care of.

Usually, the cheapest option is to pump this water back into the ground. However, as oil and gas companies are running out of good spots, which pushes up costs and earthquake risks, water needs to be cleaned.

That's where Aris comes in.

Founded a decade ago, the company operates through two primary revenue streams: Produced Water Handling and Water Solutions.

As we can see below, its integrated pipeline network, which covers roughly 745 miles (550 miles of which are large-diameter pipelines) and 66 produced water handling facilities, including 23 recycling facilities, positions the company as a leader in water management for the energy sector in the Permian.

As we can see below, it caters to some of the largest operators in the region, including ConocoPhillips (COP), Chevron (CVX), Occidental Petroleum (OXY), and some of the largest remaining private players like Mewbourne.

For these companies, the company offers a wide range of services that span the entire water management cycle from collecting produced water to recycling this water, allowing it to be reused in new well completions.

In the first quarter of this year, Aris handled roughly 1.1 million barrels of water per day, with 84% of these volumes being under long-term contracts.

Moreover, the company's extensive pipeline network and 66 handling facilities provide a competitive advantage, as they enable efficient management of produced water in the Northern Delaware Basin, which is one of the best places to be in the entire Permian Basin.

As we can see below, the Northern Delaware Basin has the best breakeven prices and the biggest remaining inventory, which means it can grow output for many years to come.

On top of that, Aris is buying land, which opens up a lot more opportunities.

This is what I wrote in my May 17 article:

[...] the company is also diversifying, buying the McNeill Ranch for $45 million. This deal comes with 45,000 surface acres in Texas and New Mexico, close to the Northern Delaware Basin.

This land is perfect for water development, and it can provide future royalties from water injection, groundwater extraction, caliche sales, and other operations. Essentially, the company is somewhat 'copying' the LandBridge business model this way.

When it comes to this deal, I believe we have only scratched the surface of what this company is capable of in the Permian Basin, as it has increasing pricing power due to exploding water volumes, recycling assets, top-tier infrastructure in place, and a healthy balance sheet to pursue growth like land additions.

Although Aris has a B+ rating, which is a total 'junk' rating, the balance sheet is healthy, as controversial as that may sound. It has a leverage ratio of a mere 2.2x EBITDA, below its 2.5-3.5x range. It also has roughly $350 million in liquidity and no maturities until 2030, when $500 million in senior notes is due.

We will likely see a number of upgrades in recent years, as there is no reason to rate the company this low.

It also has a dividend.

Currently yielding 2.4%, the dividend has a 43% payout ratio. The most recent hike was 33%, which shows investors are in a great spot to directly benefit from the company's success.

With that said, in the current environment, Aris won't be able to double. As long as oil trades in the low-$60 range, we won't see any output growth in the Permian Basin.

However, $60 WTI isn't sustainable. On a longer-term basis, I expect a steep increase in oil prices, likely pushing a lot more capital into the Delaware Basin.

This is reflected in its valuation. Six months ago, analysts expected $2.70 in 2027 EPS. Now, that number is $1.80. The good news is that this still implies 14-15% EPS growth in both 2026 and 2027.

Personally, I expect to see at least $2.70 in EPS by 2027, as current oil prices are simply not sustainable. A return to these levels would imply a stock price of roughly $50, using an 18x multiple.

This implies slightly more than 100% upside.

Even if I'm wrong, longer-term returns should be elevated, as the water business has secular growth, which makes the risk/reward so good.

Boise Cascade Company (BCC) - A Construction Supply Chain Gem

This year, I put Boise Cascade on my watchlist, as it's a company that would make a lot of sense in my portfolio due to its position as a major supply chain player in the construction industry.

Its footprint includes two sawmills, five plants producing engineered wood products, 11 plywood/veneer plants, and 38 distribution facilities that distribute these wood products and countless other supplies from third-party vendors.

While I like the company's strong supply chain due to its own sawmills, I mainly care about its distribution segment, which generates the overwhelming majority of its revenues.

The company distributes a wide range of building materials, including the aforementioned engineered wood products, commodity products, and general line products, to dealers, home improvement centers, and other customers.

Boise Cascade is so large that 13% of all multi-family I-joists are provided by the company. It also has a 40% market share in the production of laminated veneer lumber, making it one of America's most critical building material producers.

This also makes it dependent on the housing market, a market that has been undersupplied by 1.3 million units since the Great Financial Crisis, as more than a decade of underbuilding has done a number on affordability.

Unfortunately, right now, these tailwinds don't matter, as elevated rates and economic uncertainty have pressured the market. In 1Q25, the company noted a 2% decline in total housing starts and 6% lower single-family housing starts.

These headwinds pressured both total volumes and pricing power, which explains why this company has seen a drop of more than 40% in its stock price, pushing its market cap down to $3.4 billion.

While this is bad news for existing investors, it's great news for everyone looking to start a position or add to an existing one.

For starters, the dividend yield has now improved to 0.9%.

This dividend has a payout ratio of just 10% and a five-year CAGR of 16.9%.

However, the best thing is its special dividend.

Last year, the company paid a $5.00 special dividend, which would bring the current annualized yield to 6.5%.

While this year's special dividend could be lower due to an expected EPS decline from $9.57 in 2024 to $6.40 in 2025, the future remains bright.

Currently, BCC trades at just 10.8x earnings, a mile below its long-term average of 15.0x. While this year is expected to see 33% lower EPS, analysts expect close to 20% EPS growth in 2026 and 2027.

However, this is based on substantial EPS revisions. Six months ago, analysts expected $12.90 in 2027 EPS. Now, that number is 25% lower.

A return to these expectations could provide a fair stock price target of $194, nearly 120% above the current price.

While this may seem like a lot, the company, which is very volatile, has a history of steep returns, with the most recent three uptrends returning 260%, 290%, and 150%, respectively.

Just like ARIS, BCC is on my watchlist.

My only concern is its cyclical profile, as I have added a lot of cyclical stocks to my portfolio this year. Given that I have building exposure through Carlisle Companies (CSL), the Home Depot (HD), and railroads, I have not been in a rush to buy more exposure.

Needless to say, my main point is that BCC is a great company with a realistic outlook for >100% returns during the next upswing.

Takeaway

Investing in stocks isn't just a good idea. I believe it's one of the most reliable ways to build wealth over time. Maybe even *the* most reliable way.

The data backs it up.

Since 1872, the U.S. market has delivered a 7.1% annualized return (after inflation), doubling purchasing power roughly every decade.

The best thing is that there has never been a 20-year period without gains.

But I don't settle for average.

That's why my portfolio focuses on high-conviction, higher-risk plays like Texas Pacific Land and LandBridge, where I chase elevated returns without taking unnecessarily high risks.

Hence, in this article, I focused on three wild cards with serious doubling potential:

  • UnitedHealth: The beaten-down healthcare giant is trading at just 13x earnings, with a path to a lot of upside if it reclaims its historical valuation.
  • Aris Water Solutions: The Permian Basin water play with infrastructure and land assets is poised to capitalize on rising demand for water services.
  • Boise Cascade: This company is a cyclical construction gem trading at a deep discount, with a history of explosive rebounds whenever housing demand improves.

These aren't the safest bets on the market, but I think the risk/reward makes them great investments for investors who don't shy away from elevated volatility.

data.title: BGX: Not Exploiting Short Opportunities And Likely To Cut Further
data.description:
The Blackstone Long-Short Credit Income Fund (NYSE:BGX) is a closed-end fund that provides income-oriented investors with a fairly unique way to achieve their goals. On the surface, this fund is a debt fund that invests primarily in floating-rate loans and bonds. However, the difference between this fund and most other bond funds is that the Blackstone Long-Short Credit Income Fund also has the ability to go short whenever conditions warrant it. This feature allows the Blackstone Long-Short Credit Fund to be one of the few closed-end funds that can benefit from rising long-term interest rates.

The ability to benefit from rising long-term interest rates is something that could prove to be very desirable right now. As everyone reading this is by now no doubt well aware, on Friday, Moody’s officially downgraded the United States to Aa1, a level below the Aaa rating that it previously held. Moody’s was the only rating agency that still had a Aaa rating on the United States, as Standard & Poor’s downgraded the United States Federal government back in 2011, and Fitch downgraded it in 2023. Thus, by itself, this was likely not a surprise, but it could be having an impact on the bond market. This is evident in the incredibly bad twenty-year U.S. Treasury auction that took place yesterday, which saw yields increase to 5.047%, the highest level since late 2023 in the wake of the Moody’s downgrade. The ten-year U.S. Treasury yield has also been rising over the past few weeks, as shown here:

Bond prices move inversely to yields, so when the yields are rising, bond prices are falling. As most domestic bonds move with a direct correlation to U.S. Treasuries, this means that the prices of many bonds are falling. This is not a particularly good thing for most bond funds that are hoping to earn a certain level of capital gains from the assets in their portfolios. However, the fact that the Blackstone Long-Short Credit Income Fund can go short bonds, it can actually earn capital gains whether bond prices rise or fall. We can clearly see how this could be a very real advantage right now.

Yield Discussion

One of the defining characteristics of most closed-end debt funds is that they tend to have very high yields. The Blackstone Long-Short Credit Income Fund is no exception to this, as the fund boasts an 8.47% yield at the current share price. As we can see here, this is a significantly higher yield than any of the major domestic debt indices:

Index/ETF

Current Yield

Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index (AGG)

3.86%

Bloomberg U.S. High Yield Very Liquid Index (JNK)

6.68%

Bloomberg US Floating Rate Note < 5 Yrs. Index (FLOT)

5.43%

The highest-yielding of these indices right now is the Bloomberg U.S. High Yield Very Liquid Index, which tracks the performance of junk bonds issued by American companies. This index has a yield that is 179 basis points lower than that of the Blackstone Long-Short Credit Income Fund, which is a fairly large difference. After all, this means that the Blackstone Long-Short Credit Income Fund will provide an additional $17,900 in annual income on a one-million-dollar investment than would the junk bond index. That additional income could pay for quite a lot.

Unfortunately, though, the Blackstone Long-Short Credit Income Fund does not have an especially large yield advantage over that of similar closed-end funds. We can see that in this peer comparison:

Fund Name

Morningstar Classification

Current Yield

Blackstone Long-Short Credit Income Fund

Fixed Income – Taxable-Senior Loans

8.47%

Ares Dynamic Credit Allocation Fund (ARDC)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Senior Loans

9.68%

BlackRock Debt Strategies Fund (DSU)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Senior Loans

11.40%

Eaton Vance Senior Income Trust (EVF)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Senior Loans

9.36%

Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund (JQC)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Senior Loans

12.39%

Pioneer Floating Rate Fund (PHD)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Senior Loans

9.28%

abrdn Income Credit Strategies Fund (ACP)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Senior Loans

15.90%

As we can immediately see, the yield of the Blackstone Long-Short Credit Income Fund is quite a bit lower than that of its peers. It is, however, worth noting that this fund is not perfectly comparable to all of the funds on this list. First of all, several of these funds invest exclusively in floating-rate debt, but as we will see in this article, the Blackstone Long-Short Credit Income Fund can invest in both fixed-rate and floating-rate securities (although the majority of its portfolio is invested in floating-rate assets). This is important because the yields of some short-term securities are higher than those of longer-term assets. We can see this in the current yield curve:

This chart shows the yields of U.S. Treasury securities of various maturities as of the market close on May 21, 2025. As we can see, the yields of one-year to five-year U.S. Treasuries are lower than the yields of anything that matures within the next six months. The yields of the very long-dated notes and bonds (ten-year and beyond) are much higher than short-term securities, but many closed-end bond funds do not invest in anything with a maturity date that is over a decade in the future. As such, despite the recent steepening of the yield curve and the removal of the inversion that existed in the 2022 to 2024 period, some bond portfolios will still have lower yields than floating-rate debt portfolios, depending on the length of the bonds in the bond fund. This is important because it could signify a situation where the bonds held by the Blackstone Long-Short Credit Income Fund are actually dragging down the effective yield from what it would have if the fund’s portfolio were entirely invested in floating-rate securities, as some of the other funds shown above are.

Recent Performance

We previously discussed the Blackstone Long-Short Credit Income Fund on October 25, 2024. At the time, the fund had just cut its distribution from $0.1050 per share in August to $0.1010 per share for September, October, and November 2024. I predicted that these distribution cuts would continue going forward, and this proved to be correct. As we can see here, the Blackstone Long-Short Credit Income Fund has cut its distribution twice more since that time, taking it down to $0.0860 per share monthly:

The current distribution is the smallest one that this fund has had since November 2022, which clearly shows the negative impact that the Federal Reserve’s interest rate cuts have had on it. The Federal Reserve’s 100 basis points of interest rate cuts late last year have also had a negative impact on the income of just about every other floating-rate closed-end fund in the market, but some of them have oddly not cut their distributions yet. This is entirely unsustainable, and we will probably see most of them cut significantly in the coming months. The fact, then, that the Blackstone Long-Short Credit Income Fund has already cut its payout could actually give it an advantage over its peers. Basically, this fund may not suffer to the same extent as its peers in terms of net asset value deterioration or share price declines once the Federal Reserve resumes its interest rate cuts, since it has already cut its distribution.

As a general rule, the share price of closed-end funds usually declines in response to a distribution cut. As such, we can probably expect this fund to have experienced some pain in response to its recent string of distribution cuts.

This assumption proves to be correct, as shares of the Blackstone Long-Short Credit Income Fund have declined by 3.33% since the last time that we discussed this fund:

As we can clearly see in the chart above, this fund performed significantly worse than either the domestic investment-grade or the domestic junk bond indices, which is mostly because its share price declined much more than the indices during each of the declines in the price of domestic junk bonds. When we consider that this fund employs leverage as part of its investment strategy, that is not particularly surprising. After all, leverage increases both gains and losses whenever they occur.

The fund naturally did somewhat better in terms of total return, which is not surprising considering its very high yield relative to the indices. We can see that here:

As we can see, the Blackstone Long-Short Credit Income Fund provided its investors with a 1.12% total return since the last time that we discussed this. While this is better than the domestic investment-grade bond index, it still trails the performance of both domestic junk bonds and domestic floating-rate securities. Once again, this is not surprising considering that those indices have much higher yields than investment-grade bonds, and the fund has not had enough time for its even higher yield to allow its total return to surpass that of the indices.

For the most part then, the predictions that I laid out for this fund in the previous article have generally proven to be correct. However, I am certain that potential investors want to know if the worst is behind it and if this fund could be a good place to park money today in order to profit in the future. Let us have a look at it and attempt to figure that out.

About The Fund

The fund’s website states that the Blackstone Long-Short Credit Income Fund has the primary objective of providing its investors with current income, which makes sense for a debt fund. After all, this is how the majority of debt securities deliver their investment returns. This is particularly true for floating-rate securities, which tend to be almost perfectly flat over most periods of time. As we can see here, the price of the iShares Floating Rate Bond ETF has been very stable over the past decade:

As shown, in terms of price, with the notable exception of a short period of time right around the outbreak of the COVID-19 pandemic, the price of the floating-rate securities in the index rarely changed much. In fact, the COVID-19 sell-off was the only event that moved the price more than 1.5% from its starting point. However, the same index delivered a 28.65% total return over the period. Total return is the combination of price return and distributions, so this means that essentially all of the index’s investment return came from the distributions that correspond to current income.

We see this exact same thing if we look at the Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index, which tracks domestic junk bonds:

As shown, the price level of the domestic junk bond index has actually fallen by 19.67% over the past decade. This is not surprising considering that interest rates in general rose over that period. While this price performance is disappointing, we see that the index also delivered a 43.21% total return. Thus, this means that the distributions were sufficient to fully offset all of the share price decline and still give the investors in the index a positive total return. Thus, once again, all of the net investment returns that were delivered by junk bonds over the past ten years came in the form of current income.

This is important because of the strategy used by the Blackstone Long-Short Credit Income Fund. The fund’s website states that:

BGX must hold no less than 70% of its Managed Assets in first- and second-lien floating rate loans.

As we just saw, floating-rate loan securities deliver essentially all of their investment returns in the form of direct payments to the owners of the securities. As the Blackstone Long-Short Credit Income Fund is required to hold at least 70% of the total value of its portfolio in these securities, we can expect that the majority of its investment returns will come in the form of direct payments as opposed to capital gains.

The fund is currently holding well above 70% of its assets in floating-rate securities. The fund’s monthly update states that 85% of the fund’s assets are invested in floating-rate debt:

This chart specifically states that 78.9% of the fund’s assets are invested in secured loans, and an additional 7.3% of its assets invested in collateralized loan obligations. One of the defining characteristics of both types of securities is that they have floating interest rates. Thus, between the fund’s secured loans and collateralized loan obligations, the Blackstone Long-Short Credit Income Fund is sitting on about 86.2% of its assets in floating-rate securities. This is obviously the overwhelming majority of the fund’s total assets.

The fact that this fund’s assets are largely invested in floating-rate securities could prove problematic going forward. This is because the Federal Reserve is still expected to cut interest rates over the next few years despite holding off for the past three meetings. Alongside its meeting minutes from the May 2025 meeting, the Federal Open Market Committee released its projections for the likely forward path of the federal funds rate. Here it is:

This projection calls for an effective federal funds rate of 3.9% at the end of 2025, 3.4% at the end of 2026, and 3.1% at the end of 2027:

Year-End

Effective Federal Funds Rate

2025

3.9%

2026

3.4%

2027

3.1%

This would require that the Federal Reserve cut the federal funds rate by fifty basis points this year, fifty basis points next year, and 25 basis points in 2027. As of right now, nobody on the Federal Open Market Committee is projecting a rate increase at all in the next three years.

As we can see in the price chart of the floating-rate note index above, the rise in interest rates that occurred over the 2022 to 2024 period did not cause the price of the securities in the index to rise. Likewise, the interest rate cuts in late 2024 had no discernible impact on the price of the securities. Thus, we can conclude that the Federal Open Market Committee’s intentions to reduce interest rates over the next three years will not have a positive impact on the price of these securities. In contrast, falling interest rates have a negative impact on floating-rate securities because their coupon payments go down, but they do not deliver a commensurate increase in price. Thus, the vast majority of the fund’s assets will be negatively impacted should the Federal Reserve actually deliver on its current intentions.

With that said, there have been some predictions by the market recently that the Federal Reserve will not be able to keep interest rates down. As we saw in the introduction, the current yields of the ten-year U.S. Treasury note and longer-dated notes point to a higher federal funds rate in the future than what exists currently, and even the seven-year note could be suggesting a higher future federal funds rate.

With that said, the federal funds futures market believes that the most likely scenario is that the Federal Reserve will cut by 50 basis points in 2025:

The market is also projecting that fifty more basis points of cuts is the most likely outcome for the full-year 2026 period:

Interestingly, though, we do see that there are some market participants who are predicting zero cuts in either 2025 or 2026. There is also a 0.1% probability assigned to a rate hike in 2026. Regardless, though, the signs are still very much pointing towards the market agreeing with the Federal Open Market Committee that the central bank is not yet done cutting interest rates and that more will be coming down the pike. This is not a good sign for floating-rate securities, and the Blackstone Long-Short Credit Income Fund has a lot of floating-rate securities.

Interestingly, the Blackstone Long-Short Credit Income Fund does not currently have any short positions. According to the fund’s monthly update, it has a long position of 149.4% of net assets and a short position of 0.0% of net assets:

This was a rather poor choice for the fund given the market’s action over the past month or two (since the March 31, 2025, date of the most recent monthly update). This chart shows the performance of domestic junk bonds since March 31, 2025:

As we can see, junk bonds plummeted in early April, which was largely in line with everything else in the market. They have since started to rebound, which has also been consistent with many other asset classes. After all, as we all know, junk bonds tend to exhibit a greater correlation with common stocks than do investment-grade bonds because the ability of junk bond issuers to service their debt is more dependent on the strength of the economy than is the case with investment-grade issuers. Junk bonds also do not benefit from the flight-to-safety trade whenever investors start to get nervous about the state of the economy. However, we can still see that domestic junk bonds are down since the start of April. The Blackstone Long-Short Credit Income Fund had an opportunity to make some money here by going short junk bonds, but for some reason, it failed to do so. Indeed, since I first initiated coverage of the Blackstone Long-Short Credit Income Fund in late 2023, it has been a very rare occurrence for the fund to have much in the way of a short position. This is quite disappointing, as there have been ample times over the past few years when a short position would have proven to be profitable. This is very disappointing, and it suggests that the fund should be treated more as an ordinary hybrid floating-rate/junk bond fund as opposed to an absolute return fund, as is suggested by the long-short designation in the fund’s name.

Distribution Analysis

The Blackstone Long-Short Credit Income Fund pays a monthly distribution of $0.0860 per share ($1.0320 per share annually), which is a lower distribution than the $0.1010 per share that the fund had at the time of my previous article’s publication. This represents a 14.85% decline over the period, but it did not come all at once. As shown earlier, the fund cut its distribution in December 2024 and then cut it again in March 2025. This makes sense considering the timing of the Federal Reserve interest rate cuts since interest rates have a pretty big impact on the amount of income that this fund earns from the assets that it owns.

Let us have a look at the fund’s most recent financial statements, given that its annual report for the full-year period that ended on December 31, 2024, was released after the previous article on this fund was published.

For the twelve-month period that ended on December 31, 2024, the Blackstone Long-Short Credit Income Fund reported a net investment income of $14,992,081. The fund distributed a total of $15,631,178 to its shareholders over the period, so it was not able to fully cover its distribution with net investment income. This is quite unfortunate, as we would normally want a floating-rate debt fund to simply be distributing its net investment income to its shareholders. After all, the ability of any floating-rate fund to earn capital gains is very limited due to the simple fact that floating-rate securities do not change in price to any significant degree. Floating-rate securities certainly do not fluctuate to the extent of bonds or common stocks, so nearly all of the investment returns that a fund such as the Blackstone Long-Short Credit Income Fund earns will come in the form of net investment income.

With that said, the fund did manage to earn a very small amount of capital gains during the period. We can see that here:

Reporting Item

Amount

Net investment income

$14,992,081

Net realized gain/(loss)

($700,450)

Net change in unrealized appreciation/(depreciation) on Investment securities

$2,017,641

Net Increase in Net Assets Attributable to Common Shares from Operations

$16,309,272

As $16,309,272 is higher than the $15,631,178 that the fund distributed over the full-year period, it did manage to fully cover its distribution. However, it was only able to do so because of its net unrealized gains. That could prove to be problematic simply because unrealized gains could be erased by market volatility.

It is possible that these unrealized gains have been erased by the market volatility that we have seen year-to-date. This chart shows the net asset value of the Blackstone Long-Short Credit Income Fund from December 31, 2024 (the closing date of the most recent annual report) through today:

As we can see, the fund’s net asset value has declined by 1.06% since the closing date of its most recent annual report. This tells us that the Blackstone Long-Short Credit Income Fund has failed to cover its distributions since the start of this year. This means that either the net unrealized gains that allowed it full coverage of the distribution last year have been partially erased, the fund is currently distributing too much money, or it needs to cut its distribution once again. None of the three possibilities is particularly good for income-focused investors.

Valuation

Shares of the Blackstone Long-Short Credit Income Fund are currently trading at a 7.19% discount on net asset value. This is a more attractive price than the 5.69% discount that shares of this fund have had on average over the past month.

However, the Blackstone Long-Short Credit Income Fund has historically had a more attractive price than it has today. Over the past three years, shares of the Blackstone Long-Short Credit Income Fund have traded at discounts ranging from a 1.30% discount to a 15.55% discount on net asset value. The average price over the period was a 9.51% discount on net asset value, which is more attractive than the price that the fund has right now.

Here is how the current price of the Blackstone Long-Short Credit Income Fund compares to its peers:

Fund Name

Premium/(Discount)

Blackstone Long-Short Credit Income Fund

(7.19%)

Ares Dynamic Credit Allocation Fund

(2.64%)

BlackRock Debt Strategies Fund

(5.38%)

Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund

(7.12%)

Pioneer Floating Rate Fund

(2.60%)

abrdn Income Credit Strategies Fund

(5.91%)

It is very difficult to make a case for any floating-rate fund right now, given the near certainty that the distributions and share prices of all of these funds will likely decline going forward due to the Federal Reserve’s stated intention to ease monetary policy. However, the Blackstone Long-Short Credit Income Fund is the most attractively priced out of its peer group today. While this is somewhat nice, it is still at a price that is higher than its three-year average so it might be possible to get a better entry point by waiting for a short period of time.

Expenses

According to CEF Data, the Blackstone Long-Short Credit Income Fund has a current expense ratio of 5.20%, which is almost certainly going to seem very expensive to many investors. After all, most index funds have expense ratios of less than 1%, and even most actively managed funds have expense ratios that are far below the 5.20% of this fund. However, it is important to keep in mind that nearly all of the fund’s expenses come from the fact that it has to pay interest on the money that it has borrowed to obtain leverage. If we exclude these costs, the fund’s expense ratio drops to 1.70% of net assets. That is still expensive compared to many other funds in the market, but not obscenely so.

Here is how the expense ratio of the Blackstone Long-Short Credit Income Fund compares with that of its peers:
