data.title: TJX Companies' Earnings Review: For The First Time, Not A Bull (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
On May 21, ahead of the opening bell, TJX Companies (NYSE:TJX) reported fiscal 1Q'26 numbers that topped expectations pretty much across the board. Despite the good-looking results, the stock was down more than 2% ahead of the earnings call on seemingly not much bad news, other than a second-quarter outlook that landed a bit short of consensus, despite the full-year guidance remaining the same.

Below, I touch upon some of the key metrics delivered in the first fiscal quarter and some highlights of the earnings. Then, I present my case for why TJX no longer seems like a good stock to buy today, as I downgrade the shares of the Framingham, Massachusetts-based retailer for the first time.

On TJX's Fiscal 1Q'26 Results

The table below, provided by Seeking Alpha's news team, sums up very well TJX's performance in 1Q and expectations for the next several months on key indicators relative to the Street's expectations. Revenues of $13.1 billion grew 5% YOY on the back of better-than-anticipated comps of 3%. On sales, it caught my attention that every business division across every major geographic segment grew comps by at least 2% (Marmaxx US) and by as much as 5% (TJX International), suggesting broad top-line strength.

Gross margin looked a bit softer YOY, 29.5% vs. last year's 30.0%, but primarily due to unfavorable inventory hedges that concern me very little, if at all. With SG&A relative to revenues increasing slightly against 2024 levels, a pretax profit margin of 10.3% looked uninspiring against last year's 11.1%. However, considering the EPS beat, I believe that most of this margin deterioration was largely anticipated.

In the earnings call, the management team sounded very upbeat, to say the least. CEO Ernie Herrman spoke of a strong start to the second fiscal quarter and good availability of merchandise. He claims that TJX is doing fine regarding the tariff challenges and that the company's 21,000 vendors across 100 countries give TJX enough levers to offset potential inventory snags. He suggested that potential price hikes are on the table, depending on what other retailers choose to do.

All the above, however, was not enough to move the stock meaningfully in the right direction during the trading session. I suspect that the market has some of the same fears that I do, which is the impact that even small increases in merchandise cost driven by tariffs could have on profits.

Here's a quick back-on-the-envelope calculation: for every 1% increase in the cost of merchandise that is not matched by an increase in price (assume volume remains the same, and that cost of sales is 100% variable), I estimate that the operating margin takes a hit of nearly 70 bps and annual EPS drops by more than 25 cents or 6%. Assuming valuation compression in this case, it is not hard to imagine a 10% share price drop driven by what might look like de minimis changes to the top half of the P&L.

On TJX Stock

As much as possible, I try to find "that special something" about a company and stock that most investors don't seem to be talking about as much – an edge if you will. But now, when it comes to TJX, I believe that what you see is what you get: a company whose revenue and earnings growth have slowed down ahead of a higher-tariff period that could be challenging for margins, even if the management team does not seem too concerned about it.

Despite these worries, the stock still trades at a rich valuation that is hovering at or near three-year highs, as depicted below, whether one considers an EPS, EBITDA, or FCF multiple.

For a long time, my bull thesis on TJX was grounded primarily on the idea that this company and stock could behave (and have behaved, in a few instances) countercyclical to the business and equity market cycles. This is, after all, a play on discounted or off-price consumption, which does not always behave negatively to economic deceleration and loss of purchasing power.

Take the 2007-2009 crisis, for example. Sure, TJX stock sank as much as 50% or more from top to bottom, as did the S&P 500 (SPY). But during the full 36-month period, as shown below, TJX stock gained 34% against the benchmark's -16% loss, reflecting in part:

  • A 15% increase in trailing twelve-month revenues to $19.7 billion
  • A 20% increase in trailing twelve-month gross profits to $4.9 billion
  • A 31% increase in trailing twelve-month op profits to $1.7 billion
  • An operating margin expansion from the mid-7% to the mid-8%

TJX stock could continue to be a market beater in the future, particularly through periods of "traditional" economic turbulence that are characterized by low GDP growth, increasing unemployment, low inflation, and sinking interest rates. But I am afraid that the risks this time could be more challenging for TJX to navigate, as they include the potential for stagflation and pricing power pressure caused by the US-global trade war.

Revenue growth is not even the biggest problem for TJX, in my view, as it could continue to be supported by treasure-hunt shopping and a loyal consumer base. But with margins being a question mark for me, coupled with a forward P/E at nearly 30x, I am afraid that TJX might no longer be the "anti-gravity stock" that attracted me to it in 2018.

I downgrade TJX for the first time ever, from buy to neutral.
